coredata.dc:title: Development of a novel methodology for in vivo quantification of N/O/S-containing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons located on the epidermis of mangrove roots using graphene quantum dots as a fluorescence quencher
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruilong
Wang, Shaopeng
Wang, Yinghui
Yu, Kefu
coredata.dc:description: A novel approach for in vivo determination of typical N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots was developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a fluorescence quencher. The decreasing fluorescence intensity from GQDs was attributed to the amount of N/O/S-containing PAHs introduced onto the epidermis of mangrove roots. The linear ranges of the proposed method were 10.3–980ngg−1, 9.5–1350ngg−1 and 7.8–1200ngg−1 for DBF, DBT and CAR located on the epidermis of K. obovata roots, respectively. This method was also shown to be valid for quantifying the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the root epidermis in the presence of heavy metal (10mmolL−1) and dissolved organic matter (1mgL−1 C). Moreover, the death rates of epidermal cells were almost unchanged (p >0.05) after acquiring the fluorescence spectra, which is superior to the previously reported LITRF method with which the cell death rates increased to 42.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Root
In vivo
N/O/S-containing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo determination of the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of mangrove roots
Effects of heavy metals (HM) and dissolved organic matter (DOM) on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs
Death of the epidermal cells caused by the excitation light of LITRF and the proposed method
The possibility of determining, in vivo, the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of mangrove roots using the fluorescence spectra of GQDs
The implications of auto-fluorescence of root epidermis for the acquisition of GQD fluorescence spectra
The spatiotemporal distribution of GQDs on the mangrove roots
The cell death rates after acquiring the fluorescence spectra of GQDs on the epidermis of mangrove roots
Analytical merits of the proposed method
Influence of the HM and DOM on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs
In vivo measurement of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of contaminated wild mangrove roots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 91428203 , No. 41673105 , No. 41473118 ) and Guangxi Ba-Gui Fellowship (No. 2014A010 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The percentages of stainless, single and heavily stained cells before (No. 1) and after acquiring the fluorescence spectra by the LITRF (No. 2) and in vivo (No. 3) methods.

(a) The fluorescence spectra for the GQDs on the epidermis of K. obovata roots. Concentrations of CAR adsorbed from 1 to 7 were 0, 50, 100, 150, 250, 350, 500ngg−1, respectively. (b) A calibration graph of CAR on the epidermis of K. obovata roots under the optimal operational conditions.

The F 0/F values of GQDs induced by the presence of DBF, DBT and CAR (500ngg−1) with the presence of Pb2+ (10mmolL−1) and DOM (1mgL−1 C and 10mgL−1 C) (a) K. obovata; (b) A. marina; (c) A. corniculata.
Analytical merits of the proposed method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Detection limit of the method, which was calculated by 3SB/m, where ‘SB’ is the standard deviation of the blank, and ‘m’ is the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: y represents the F 0/F values of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: x represents the concentrations of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of wild mangrove roots achieved by the proposed method a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each wild mangrove root contains 500ngg−1 of Phen, Pyr and B[a]P.

head.ce:title: Development of a novel methodology for in vivo quantification of N/O/S-containing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons located on the epidermis of mangrove roots using graphene quantum dots as a fluorescence quencher

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Marine Sciences, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China
Coral Reef Research Center of China, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Coral Reef Research Center of China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Laboratory on the Study of Coral Reefs in the South China Sea, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Laboratory on the Study of Coral Reefs in the South China Sea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Marine Sciences, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel approach for in vivo determination of typical N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots was developed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a fluorescence quencher. The decreasing fluorescence intensity from GQDs was attributed to the amount of N/O/S-containing PAHs introduced onto the epidermis of mangrove roots. The linear ranges of the proposed method were 10.3–980ngg−1, 9.5–1350ngg−1 and 7.8–1200ngg−1 for DBF, DBT and CAR located on the epidermis of K. obovata roots, respectively. This method was also shown to be valid for quantifying the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the root epidermis in the presence of heavy metal (10mmolL−1) and dissolved organic matter (1mgL−1 C). Moreover, the death rates of epidermal cells were almost unchanged (p >0.05) after acquiring the fluorescence spectra, which is superior to the previously reported LITRF method with which the cell death rates increased to 42.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Parent polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are of particular concern due to their carcinogenicity, mutagenicity and toxicity to biota (Salvo et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2013). Some recent studies have shown that the N/O/S-containing PAHs possess toxicities that are comparable to those of their parent compounds (Cachada et al., 2016; Andersson and Achten, 2015). In addition, unlike the parent PAHs commonly investigated, important factors in the determination of N/O/S-containing PAHs combined with root epidermal cell walls/membranes may also include n-ᴨ interactions, electrostatic interactions and steric stabilization, and thus need special investigation (Sun and Zhou, 2016; Chen et al., 2015).

Root uptake of PAHs has long been considered an important pathway for human exposure through the food chain (Gao and Zhu, 2004). To explore the uptake mechanisms, considerable efforts have been made to investigate the PAHs' uptake coefficients (K f) and their relationships with the compositions of roots (mainly the functional groups of lipids and carbohydrates) (Naidoo and Naidoo, 2016; Zhang and Zhu, 2009). Nevertheless, Jiao et al. confirmed that portions of PAHs are retained on the epidermis of roots, and only approximately 60% of the PAHs actually enter into the root; their analysis used a sequential extraction (CaCl2, methanol and ASE extraction) combined with GC–MS (Jiao et al., 2007) and made it necessary to re-determine the K f values and re-evaluate the roles of composition on root uptake of PAHs. Therefore, an accurate determination of PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots was critical to an improved understanding of the root uptake and further translocation processes.

However, the sequential extraction method destroys not only the original form of the PAHs but also the root compositions (lipid/carbohydrates/protein) and cell membranes, which affect the uptake extent of PAHs and their locations (epidermis, xylem and so on) (Chen and Schnoor, 2009; Li and Ma, 2016; Zhan et al., 2015). In other words, the results acquired by the sequential extraction method cannot represent the retention of PAHs in the epidermis of intact and living roots. Currently, as far as we know, there exists no PAH quantification method that meets the requirements of in vivo determination of the retention of PAHs on the epidermis of roots.

As PAHs have high fluorescence quantum yields, both the solid surface fluorescence (SSF) and fiber-optic fluorescence (FOF) spectroscopic methods have been established to determine the PAHs adsorbed on leaf surfaces in situ (Wang et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2011). As they are limited by the sensitivity of the fluorescence instruments, these methods are not suitable for investigating the location and transportation of trace concentrations of PAHs on mangrove leaves. With the application of laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) spectroscopy, the low sensitivities that hampered SSF and FOF methods were resolved by Sun et al., as the detection limit of the LIF method is lower by a factor of 10–30 (Sun et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2016). This method has been subsequently used to investigate the depuration of PAHs adsorbed onto the leaf surfaces of living mangrove seedlings.

Unfortunately, compared with the mangrove leaf, the strong auto-fluorescence of mangrove root epidermal tissues combined with the relatively low fluorescence signals of PAHs results in a low signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), rendering the LIF method useless for in situ monitoring of the PAHs located on the epidermal tissues of mangrove roots (Cheng et al., 2015). To suppress the background fluorescence signal, the in situ laser-induced nanosecond time-resolved fluorescence (LITRF) spectroscopic method was established in our previous reports (Li et al., 2017; Li et al., 2015), with which the S/N values improve by a factor of 3–4 for fluorene adsorbed onto the epidermal tissues of mangrove roots. However, further studies showed that the optimal excitation wavelength for the LITRF method determination of nearly all the PAHs was in the UV range (250–350nm), and the high photon energy of UV light could result in the production of hydroxyl radical (OH) and thereby cause negative effects on many aspects, including changing the normal physiological functions of the root cells, which will further induce changes in the locations of PAHs on root tissues (Verdaguer et al., 2017; Robson et al., 2015; Le Gall et al., 2015).

As the excitation wavelength of two-photon laser confocal scanning microscopy (TPLCSM) equates to a two-photon adsorption wavelength, this method, by using visible instead of UV light, minimizes the negative effects of the excitation light and realizes in vivo monitoring of the translocations and degradation of PAHs in microalga (Feng et al., 2017; Li and Chen, 2014; Niu et al., 2016). Nevertheless, the in vivo TPLCSM method, if not in combination with high sensitivity fluorescence spectroscopy, is unable to quantify the PAHs located on the epidermal tissues of mangrove roots (Tan et al., 2017).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted widespread interest in the quantification of organic/inorganic substances, sensors and biosensors (Park et al., 2015; Benitez-Martinez et al., 2014; Ji et al., 2016). Among these fluorescence sensors, in theory, the quantification methods based on GQDs fluorescence quenching have the potential ability to avoid the detrimental effects on root for the longer excitation wavelength of GQDs (visible light). The objective of this study was, therefore, to use GQDs as a fluorescence quencher to achieve in vivo determination of PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots. Dibenzothiophene (DBT), carbazole (CAR) and dibenzofuran (DBF) possess the same number of rings and were selected to represent S-containing, N-containing and O-containing PAH compounds, respectively.

DBT, CAR and DBF with a purity of 99.9% were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. (UK). All of the other chemicals used in the study were analytical reagents (A.R.) obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). The GQDs used in this study were purchased from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd. (USA), with the particle diameter <5nm (the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths in aqueous solutions were 350nm and 462nm, respectively).

The hypocotyls of K. obovata, A. marina and A. corniculata were collected from the Yunxiao mangrove swamp located in the Zhangjiang River Estuary, southeast China, and were then cultivated in a sand bed for 12months. The other cultural conditions were the same as previously reported (Li et al., 2016). The fluorescence spectrometer purchased from the Edinburgh Instruments Co Ltd. (FLS-980, UK) was used to acquire the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs retained on the epidermal tissues of mangrove roots. The parameters of this instrument are as follows: Excitation wavelength, 350nm; Emission wavelengths, 400–550nm; Slit width, 2.00nm; Dwell time, 0.500ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo determination of the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of mangrove roots
ce:section.ce:para: Healthy K. obovata, A. marina and A. corniculata with similar length roots were selected and then cleaned with tap water and Milli-Q water three times each to remove silt, sand and sediment. Similar to our previous report (Li et al., 2017), after the roots had been air-dried, the special zones, which include the divisional and elongation zones, were selected in the mangrove roots. Then, the mangrove root was immersed in 10mL of a 100mgmL−1 GQD solution for 30min. To obtain calibration curves, different concentrations of the CAR, DBF and DBT acetone solutions were slowly introduced onto the special zones of mangrove root epidermis using a 50μL transferpettor. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs were acquired using the FLS-980, and the statistical results of nine measurements were used to calculate the calibration curves and the corresponding correlation coefficients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of heavy metals (HM) and dissolved organic matter (DOM) on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several groups of N/O/S containing PAHs contaminated K. obovata roots were prepared in order to evaluate the effects of HM and DOM on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by the method described in Section 2.2. Then, the contaminated roots were fully immersed into the aqueous solutions containing the inherent DOM (1 and 10mgL−1 C) and HM (10mmolL−1). The inherent DOM that originated from mangrove sediment was collected, filtered and acidified by the same procedures described by Gao et al. (2007). The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots were acquired at 120h using the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Death of the epidermal cells caused by the excitation light of LITRF and the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the impacts of the excitation light on the physiological status of the roots, the quantity of dead epidermal cells was determined by Evans blue staining, which only enters dead cells that have a freely permeable plasma membrane. The experimental procedures were similar to those reported by Mergemann et al. (Mergemann and Sauter, 2000). Briefly, the mangrove roots were first immersed into 2% (w/v) Evans blue for 3min. The root samples were subsequently washed with tap water and Milli-Q water, three times each. Finally, wide-field fluorescence microscopy was used to examine the Evans blue staining patterns from which the death rates of mangrove root epidermal cells were calculated.
The mean and standard deviation of replicates were calculated and compared by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) tests. All statistical analyses were run using Origin version 9.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possibility of determining, in vivo, the N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of mangrove roots using the fluorescence spectra of GQDs
The implications of auto-fluorescence of root epidermis for the acquisition of GQD fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Li et al. reported that the auto-fluorescence of woody root epidermis was so strong that it affected the fluorescence spectra of PAHs with the S/N value of only 8.37 (λex =266nm, λem =368nm) (Li et al., 2015). However, whether the GQD fluorescence spectra with different excitation and emission wavelengths (λex =350nm, λem =462nm) would also be affected by the auto-fluorescence remained largely unknown. Therefore, it was necessary to investigate further before acquiring the GQDs' fluorescence spectra.

In our study, as shown in Fig. S1, the S/N value of the DBF fluorescence peaks was 7.17 (λex =350nm, λem =462nm), even lower than that of PAHs on the epidermis. This low S/N value indicates that the sensitivity of fluorescence spectral methods in determining DBF is rather low, and thus the deductions of the short-lifetime fluorescence signal have to be used. After the deduction, the root auto-fluorescence signals have almost no effects on the fluorescence spectra of GQDs at the wavelength range from 400nm to 550nm (S/N increased to 82.49). These results indicated that the LITRF spectra of GQDs were immune to the effects of the auto-fluorescence signal and thus fit with our requirement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The spatiotemporal distribution of GQDs on the mangrove roots
ce:section.ce:para: Once the GQDs have been transported to tissues, the fluorescence quenching of GQDs may also include the fluorescence signals that cannot be collected by our fluorescence spectrometer (Bünting and Karlitschek, 1998). Therefore, to further explore the possibility of using GQDs as the fluorescence quencher, the distribution of GQDs on the mangrove root was investigated using the FLIM images (Shang et al., 2016), which were suitable to display the fluorescence signal of the GQDs (green to blue, 15–25ns) and the auto-fluorescence signal of the root (red to yellow, <10ns) separately (Fig. S2). Fortunately, nearly all of the GQDs were retained on the epidermis of the mangrove root and were not transported to the xylem/phloem tissues through root water and nutrient absorption pathways in 10d. Moreover, similar to the distribution of N/O/S containing PAHs, the predominant concentrations of GQDs were located on the cell walls of the epidermis. These results suggest that the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by N/O/S containing PAHs occurred at the epidermis of the mangrove root.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cell death rates after acquiring the fluorescence spectra of GQDs on the epidermis of mangrove roots
ce:section.ce:para: The rates at which excitation light induced epidermal cell death were evaluated and the results are presented in Fig. 1 . Before acquiring the fluorescence spectra of GQDs (optimal excitation wavelength: 350nm, visible light), approximately 73.4% of all epidermal cells did not show any staining, while approximate 21.1% and 5.5% displayed single and heavily stained cells, respectively. The classification of unstained, single and heavily stained cells was similar to that reported by Mergemann et al. reported (Mergemann and Sauter, 2000). After acquiring the fluorescence spectra of GQDs by the proposed method, the percentages of stainless cells showed no obvious difference (p >0.05) and actually only decreased slightly to 68.5%. However, after the LITRF method (optimal excitation wavelength: 266nm, UV light) had been applied, the reductions in the stainless cells were obvious (p <0.05), and the heavily stained cells increased significantly to 42.6% (p <0.05). These differences reveal that our proposed in vivo method was superior to the previously reported LITRF method in protecting the epidermal cells of mangrove roots from the damage caused the excitation light.
In summary, the fluorescence quenching of the GQD method has the potential to realize in vivo determinations of the N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the root epidermis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical merits of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the quenching process and further establish the in vivo method, the Stern-Volmer equation (Zhang et al., 2017; R. Chen et al., 2016) was used as follows:where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of GQDs on the epidermis of mangrove roots with and without the presence of N/O/S containiF 0/F =1+ K SV ∗ C PAHsng PAHs, respectively, K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and C PAHs is the concentration of PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove root. As shown in Fig. 2 , the quenching constant (F 0/F) at the optimal wavelength increased with the increase of CAR on the epidermis of K. obovata roots. Similar results were also obtained for DBT and DBF. Notably, strong positive correlations were found between the F 0/F values and C PAHs with the coefficients of 0.9963, 0.9883 and 0.9905 for CAR, DBT and DBF, respectively (p <0.05). These data suggest that the fluorescence quenching of GQDs by N/O/S-containing PAHs was not a dynamic quenching process but a static one. Given the presence of conjugated systems (Wang et al., 2017), this static quenching of GQDs was mainly attributed to both the π–π stacking interactions and the hydrophobic interactions between the GQDs and the N/O/S-containing PAHs.

Further studies were conducted to identify the analytical merits of the in vivo quantification method for N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots, and the results are presented in Table 1 . The linear ranges were 10.3–980ngg−1, 9.5–1350ngg−1 and 7.8–1200ngg−1, with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9905, 0.9883 and 0.9963 for DBF, DBT and CAR, respectively. The detection limits can reach as low as 1.9ngg−1, 2.5ngg−1 and 1.3ngg−1 for the DBF, DBT and CAR located on the epidermis of K. obovata roots, respectively. Similar results were also obtained for DBF, DBT and CAR located on the epidermis of A. marina and A. corniculata roots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the HM and DOM on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the location of mangrove ecosystems, the HM and DOM contents were both relatively high compared with the terrestrial soils (M. Chen et al., 2016; Clemens and Ma, 2016). Thus, the influence of these two factors on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs should be evaluated to validate whether the proposed method has potential ability to determine the N/O/S-containing PAHs on mangrove root epidermis in the field. Fortunately, no impacts on the F 0/F values of GQDs induced by the presences of DBF, DBT and CAR (500ngg−1) were observed with the addition of 10mmolL−1 Pb2+ (p >0.05, Fig. 3a). Similar results were also obtained for the addition of other common HM (Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Cd2+). This was probably because nearly all of the HM taken up by woody/mangrove roots was likely to translate to stem and leaf tissues rather than remain on the root epidermis. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs quenched by trace concentrations of HM can be considered negligible in mangrove root epidermis.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3b, the F 0/F values of GQDs induced by the presence of DOM remained constant at low concentrations of DOM (1mgL−1 C) but increased significantly to 15.88, 12.69 and 13.33 at the higher concentrations of DOM (10mgL−1 C) for (500ngg−1) DBF, DBT and CAR, respectively. The DOM that originated from the soil/sediment, as Song et al. and Zhang et al. reported (Song et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2009), includes not only the hydrophilic acids/neutrals but also the hydrophobic and amphiphilic acids/neutrals. Thus, the hydrophobic and amphiphilic fractions made the DOM much more easily adsorbed onto the root epidermis than HM was, and this explains the quenching of the GQDs' fluorescence signal at relatively high DOM concentrations. These results indicated that the proposed method was suitable for in vivo determination of the N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots under the condition of low DOM content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo measurement of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of contaminated wild mangrove roots
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability of the proposed quenching method, the determination of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of contaminated wild mangrove roots was performed and the results are presented in Table 2 . The wild K. obovata, A. marina and A. corniculata plants were collected from the Shankou Mangrove Nature Reserve of China (21°28′ N, 109°37′ E) and were briefly cleaned with tap water and Milli-Q water for three rinses each. All of the roots were spiked with N/O/S containing PAHs (25ngg−1 and 100ngg−1). Considering the potential negative effects of HM and DOM on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs, the other trace metal ions (Pb2+, Ag+, Hg2+ and Fe3+) and DOM were added in the aqueous solution. For comparison, the same samples were analyzed by the LITRF method established in previous studies (Li et al., 2017). As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of N/O/S-containing PAHs by these two methods were quite good, with the recovery of DBT, CAR and DBF on the K. obovata in the ranges of 94.0–97.6%, 101.3–105.6% and 98.8–104.9%, respectively. Moreover, the reduction of stainless cells of mangrove root epidermis after detection by the proposed method was only 62.1%, which is much higher than the previously established LITRF method (p <0.05).

Our results indicated that the direct fluorescence quenching method developed in this study has great potential for practical application for in vivo determinations of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of wild mangrove roots. However, we have to admit that the proposed method did not have the ability to determine the mixtures of N/O/S-containing PAHs on the epidermis of mangrove root, and additional work will be required to address this issue.

This study presented a novel fluorescence quenching method for in vivo determination of N/O/S-containing PAHs located on the epidermis of mangrove roots using GQDs as a quencher, a method that has almost no impact on the death rates of epidermal cells. In addition, the interferences of trace HM (10mmolL−1) and low concentrations of DOM (1mgL−1 C) can be eliminated, and the detection limits can reach as low as 0.8–3.1ngg−1. The proposed method provides us with an advanced tool to investigate in vivo the N/O/S-containing PAH uptake pathways by plants with normal physiology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 91428203, No. 41673105, No. 41473118) and Guangxi Ba-Gui Fellowship (No. 2014A010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpolbul.2017.12.040.
